We’re going to start this module with an overview of The
Relationship Building Series (RBS).
Relationship Building Series
(RBS)
www.getwsodo.com

-- 1 of 10 --

Closed captioning (subtitles) will be available for this video in the
next two days.
The Relationship Building Series
(Details)
This is probably the most important module in the Art of Email
because everything else — every downstream activity and
interaction — is affected by a Relationship Building Series.
A Relationship Building Series (RBS) is a cohesive collection of
emails based on a theme, idea, or story that serves as the
introduction for people to experience your world.
It’s important to note that a Relationship Building Series is a
www.getwsodo.com

-- 2 of 10 --

speci�c type of Soap Opera Series with a few unique
characteristics, including:
▪ A Relationship Building Series is the entry point for everyone
who enters your world.
▪ There is no selling in an RBS.
(That last point is important. Re-read it, internalize it.)
Other important characteristics of an RBS include:
▪ Each RBS has a beginning and an end.
▪ An RBS can last for days or weeks (longer in some rare
examples).
▪ Email frequency can be adjusted to match your needs and
the needs of your audience (for example, ten emails in a row
delivered daily, seven emails sent every two days, etc.).
▪ Every business needs at least one Relationship Building
Series. However, you may create more than one to match the
number of entry points into your world. (For example, one
RBS for people who opt-in vs. a different RBS for people who
buy a product immediately.)
▪ Uses narrative-driven themes and arcs as the emotional
delivery vehicle for your marketing message. The e�ect, from
a subscriber’s point of view, is a feeling of being emotionally
pulled forward, wanting to know more, bonding with the
recipient, and moving closer to their worldview.
▪ Creates a frame and establishes context that your audience
www.getwsodo.com

-- 3 of 10 --

needs to understand and internalize in order to �gure out
where you �t in their world, and how the value you have to
offer relates to them speci�cally (or, when there is
incongruence, or values don’t align, doesn’t).
An RBS “�lters” people — it pulls (and mentally tugs) at the right
people through a concept we call worldbuilding, and repels those
people who are not a good �t, people who don’t align with your
ideal customer pro�le.
It’s important to mention that a Relationship Building Series is not
a collection of random emails, or a conventional onboarding email
campaign.
A typical follow-up sequence takes on many different shapes on a
spectrum ranging from “thanks for subscribing” (with little to no
other automated interactions) on one side, to ‘indoctrination’-style
sequences somewhere in the middle, to all out “sell sell sell!” on
the other end…
Although the spectrum varies, the characteristics of a typical
onboarding series are broadly similar (and very predictable).
�. Welcome, a bit about me…
�. Check out my awesome content…
�. Here is what I can do for you…
�. I have this (or these) course(s)…
�. Make an offer (apply urgency and offer incentive to act
now)…
www.getwsodo.com

-- 4 of 10 --

Email 1, email 2, email 3, etc., on a path leading towards an
inevitable sales offer three or four or �ve emails later.
Any bonding that occurs is expressed in the �rst and maybe
second emails, then the “vice” slowly cranks, pressure is ratcheted
up — sometimes subtly, other times blatantly — towards a sale.
From the perspective of the subscriber, this feels like “push”
marketing. Each touchpoint nudges-nudges-nudges-nudges
towards an inevitable offer the subscriber knows will come.
What’s more, there’s another invisible dynamic at play, which we’ve
written about previously, and included below for context.
There’s a BIG problem with typical approaches to email
marketing…
However, the problem is invisible unless you know where to look
for it, and that’s a much BIGGER problem. Day after day, it’ll
undermine your results, like a silent thief picking your pockets in
broad daylight.
Let’s assume that you take a conventional approach to lead
generation…
Someone opts-in, receives a three-part ‘indoctrination’ or
‘welcome’ series, then a promotion starts.
Thirty days after your �rst email, your overall conversion rate is
3% (which would be considered a little better than average
www.getwsodo.com

-- 5 of 10 --

according to ‘conventional wisdom’).
What do you do?
More often than not, once the low hanging fruit has been
harvested, the answer is to focus on making the promotion
better…
▪ Add another bonus or two…
▪ Include more social proof…
▪ Emphasize bene�ts…
▪ Write more persuasive copy…
▪ Test headlines…
▪ Turn up the dial on urgency…
▪ Add another upsell…
Then, weeks later, repeat the process.
Keep squeezing the fruit as hard as you can hoping to �nd just
one more drop of juice…
But here’s the problem — all of these changes assume that the way to
improve conversion rates is to sell better.
However, the real opportunity — invisible to most marketers — is
to improve performance before the selling starts.
To do that, we focus �rst on developing relationships with our
www.getwsodo.com

-- 6 of 10 --

Example (Emergent Marketing
Series)
We’ll end this module with a look at our Relationship Building
Series.
The single entry point onto our email list is here. That page
started off as email #1, which we then repurposed as our lead-in.
audience, and that requires more attention and effort than a
three-part ‘indoctrination’ sequence.
Trust isn’t a box to check on our way to selling.
Trust is the lifeblood of our businesses.
In the Art of Email, everything begins with a Relationship
Building Series. An RBS is 5 — 10, sometimes more, emails that
establish empathy, credibility, authority (and plant the seeds for
preeminence).
A Relationship Building Series does not sell. (But it dramatically
ampli�es the effects of selling later.)
An RBS is a little bit about you, and a LOT about them — their
needs, desires, problems to solve (or avoid), experiences to pursue,
and goals to achieve.
www.getwsodo.com

-- 7 of 10 --

Someone has to resonate enough with the message articulated on
that page — and be pulled to want to subscribe, which then kicks
off our �rst email.
▪ Email 1
▪ Email 2
▪ Email 3
▪ Email 4
▪ Email 5
▪ Email 6
www.getwsodo.com

-- 8 of 10 --

▪ Email 7
Emails 1 through 6 unpack our Emergent Marketing Series,
describing who we are, what we stand for, and how we can
help our audience.
Our series “endpoint” technically is email #6, which then
mentions email #7. Email #7 is our ‘choose your own adventure,’
where we frame, then link to all of our courses, leaving the
subscriber to take a path that best matches his/her needs.
At this point every subscriber is tagged as having completed our
prerequisite EMS Relationship Building Series, at which point
they’re added to our VNL (which we call our ‘Emergent Marketing
Newsletter) loop.
At this point, the �rst step in our LEGO-building approach can
begin to be observed: RBS + VNL.
Next we get to assemble our unique AoE system in a way that
www.getwsodo.com

-- 9 of 10 --

matters to us. We could trigger any number of SPPs based on
what link(s) a subscriber clicks on as their preferred next step.
Or we could trigger an SOS (as described in the previous module),
to further deeper the relationship we’re working hard to establish
and nurture.
Or we could trigger a Showcase Series, a version of an SPP that
doesn’t explicitly sell but instead implicitly pulls people to WANT
to buy. (More about that later.)
A Relationship Building Series should be the �rst campaign you
create. You can do that now, before moving on to the next
modules. Or, continue your journey through the Art of Email and
come back to this module later.
Every email campaign you create will be better if it follows a high-
quality RBS. Take this work seriously.
Please ask questions and leave comments below.
—André & Shawn
www.getwsodo.com

-- 10 of 10 --
